title: Coordination for Soil Carbon Research and Monitoring Act
short_title: Coordination for Soil Carbon Research and Monitoring Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a new government committee to help different federal agencies work together on studying soil and how it can store carbon. The committee will be run by the Office of Science and Technology Policy and will make plans and reports for Congress about soil carbon research. The goal is to learn how soil can help fight climate change by storing carbon from the air. The committee will focus on including farmers and communities that have not had much help before in this research.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Conservation, Research, and Biotechnology.

sections.title: Section 1. Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Coordination for Soil Carbon Research and Monitoring Act.

sections.title: Section 2. Soil Carbon Research and Monitoring
sections.text:
The government will create a new committee to study how soil stores carbon. This committee will be called the Interagency Committee on Soil Carbon Research. The Office of Science and Technology Policy will lead this committee. The committee will include people from many government agencies like the Department of Agriculture, Department of Energy, Department of the Interior, Environmental Protection Agency, United States Geological Survey, National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, National Science Foundation, National Aeronautics and Space Administration, White House Environmental Justice Advisory Council, and National Institute of Standards and Technology. The leader of the Office of Science and Technology Policy can add other government agencies if needed.

The committee has several important jobs. It must create a plan for all government agencies to work together on soil carbon research. Soil carbon sequestration means the process of capturing and storing carbon in soil to help reduce greenhouse gases in the air. The committee will decide what each agency should do to help with this research and monitoring. It will also watch over smaller work groups that focus on specific topics.

The committee must talk with other government agencies and the Office of Management and Budget about planning and money for soil carbon research. Within one year after this law starts, the committee must send Congress a report about what soil carbon research is already happening. Then it must send progress reports to Congress after one year, three years, and five years.

The committee will help government agencies work together better on soil carbon monitoring, collecting data about soil carbon and greenhouse gas movements, and keeping soil carbon stored for long periods. It will support complete reporting on soil carbon activities by combining data from different agencies in a standard way. The committee will share its recommendations with soil science experts from colleges, private companies, and nonprofit groups that represent the diversity of American agriculture. One committee member will be chosen to work directly with agency leaders on soil carbon research and monitoring.

The committee can create smaller work groups to focus on specific areas of soil carbon research. These areas include Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification (which means checking and confirming that carbon storage is working properly), collecting and managing data with focus on measurement standards and new technology, basic research about how soil carbon works and soil improvements, and involving communities in research especially groups that have been historically underserved or disadvantaged.

These work groups have specific tasks. They must find out what soil carbon research and monitoring each government agency is already doing. They must identify what is missing in current soil carbon research and monitoring efforts. They must describe how agencies are already working together on soil carbon research and monitoring. They must suggest coordinated projects and budget requests to improve soil carbon research and monitoring.

The work groups must reach out to farming communities and farmers, especially those who have been historically underserved, to make sure soil carbon research includes input from people who actually work with soil. They must work with soil scientists from research institutions and universities, private companies, nonprofit organizations, and other expert groups to make sure recommendations are based on the best available science. They must also work together to create standard ways of monitoring the long term carbon and environmental effects of soil carbon research.

Congress approves spending up to ten million dollars to carry out this section.
